title: Who Is Copying Who? Part III
gist: Gave revisits his 'Who Is Copying Who?' thesis: the late-1990s bet that trade would make China more Western and democratic failed, and instead Western democracies (Canada, US, UK, Germany) adopted increasingly China-like state control during Covid, with this teaser flagging that the same convergence is now showing up on the corporate side.

insights.claim: The 1990s case for admitting China to the WTO rested on the belief that trade exposure would make China more Western and democratic, modeled on South Korea and Taiwan, which abandoned military dictatorships for representative democracy after their economies grew through integration into global supply chains.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: This historical framework is the baseline investors were sold on China engagement; its failure (per Gave) undercuts assumptions that continued economic integration with China will produce political liberalization.
insights.quote: if the Western world embraced trade with China, then, in time, China would become more “Western” and democratic
insights.locator: Back in the late 1990s, the argument

insights.claim: Contrary to the 1990s thesis, Gave argues that across his prior 'Who Is Copying Who?' papers written during Covid, it was the West that became more like China, not the other way around.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: A core recurring Gavekal macro theme: investors should track Western institutional/political convergence toward Chinese-style state control as a structural regime-risk factor, not assume liberalization is the default trajectory.
insights.quote: rather than China becoming more like the West, it often felt as if the West was becoming more like China
insights.locator: In a series of papers written during Covid

themes.theme: Covid-era evidence of Western convergence toward Chinese-style control

insights.claim: During Covid, pastors in Canada were arrested for celebrating mass, cited by Gave as evidence of Western governments restricting individual rights in the name of collective policy goals.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: Signals a structural willingness by Western governments to override individual religious freedom for public-health mandates, a precedent that could recur in future crises.
insights.quote: In Canada, pastors were arrested for celebrating mass.

insights.claim: In the US during Covid, individuals were removed from social media platforms for expressing views deemed 'wrong think', which Gave frames as milder than the UK and Germany, where people faced police visits and even arrest or imprisonment for 'wrong talk'.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: Highlights a spectrum of speech-restriction severity across Western democracies (platform deplatforming in the US vs. criminal enforcement in UK/Germany) relevant to assessing free-speech and platform-regulation risk in each jurisdiction.
insights.quote: Which was better than the UK or Germany where individuals would start to receive visits from the police, and sometimes even face arrest and imprisonment for “wrong talk”.
insights.locator: In the US, individuals were kicked off

insights.claim: Vaccines were mandated across entire Western populations during Covid regardless of an individual's preexisting immunity or religious belief, which Gave cites as a further marker of the shift away from individual-rights-based policymaking.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: Illustrates how public-health emergencies were used to justify blanket mandates overriding individual medical and religious circumstances, a template that could reappear in future health or climate emergencies.
insights.quote: Vaccines were mandated across the population, regardless of preexisting immunity, religious belief or more simply, plain common sense.
insights.locator: Vaccines were mandated across the population

insights.claim: Gave frames the Covid-era policy shift as a move in the West from treating 'the rights of the individual' as the sacrosanct guiding principle of policymaking to instead managing for the 'optimal outcome for the community'.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: A useful mental model for investors assessing regulatory and civil-liberties risk in Western markets: watch for policy justified on collective-outcome grounds as a signal of this same individual-to-collective pivot recurring outside health policy.
insights.quote: the political cursor across the West moved from holding up “the rights of the individual” as the sacrosanct shining light of policymaking, to managing for the “optimal outcome for the community”
insights.locator: With Covid, the political cursor across the West moved

themes.theme: Teased extension to the corporate world

insights.claim: Gave signals that the same 'West becoming more like China' dynamic he previously documented in politics and policy is now also showing up on the corporate side, though the mechanism is left unexplained in this teaser and reserved for the full article.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: Flags an upcoming Gavekal thesis extension worth following: if Western corporate behavior/governance is converging toward Chinese-style patterns (e.g. state-directed capital allocation, consolidation, industrial policy), that has direct implications for how investors assess Western corporate governance and competitive dynamics.
insights.quote: this “becoming more like China” is also becoming prevalent on the corporate side of things
insights.locator: But interestingly, this “becoming more like China”
